intitule: Lesson 11 Assessment: Conditional Sentences - Theory Check

enonce.texte: This exercise tests your understanding of the structure and use of Zero, Type 1, and Type 2 conditionals. Read each question carefully and apply the rules.

questions.texte: Match each conditional type (Zero, Type 1, Type 2) with its correct structure and use. Then, complete the table by explaining the time/reference of each.
questions.reponse: Zero Conditional: If + present simple, present simple - General truths/scientific facts. Type 1: If + present simple, will + infinitive - Real/possible future situations. Type 2: If + past simple, would + infinitive - Hypothetical/unreal present or future situations.

sousQuestions.texte: Identify the type of conditional: 'If you heat ice, it melts.'
Identify the type of conditional: 'If she studies hard, she will pass the BEPC exam.'
Identify the type of conditional: 'If I were the mayor of Douala, I would solve the flood problem.'

questions.texte: Explain the difference in meaning between the following two sentences. Why is the verb 'was' replaced by 'were' for all subjects in one of them?
questions.reponse: The first sentence (Type 1) expresses a real possibility: it is likely that I will have time. The second sentence (Type 2) expresses an unreal/hypothetical situation: I do not have time now, and I am imagining a different scenario. In Type 2 conditionals, the subjunctive mood requires 'were' for all subjects (I, he, she, it) instead of 'was' to show the situation is hypothetical and not real.

sousQuestions.texte: If I have time, I will help you.
If I had time, I would help you.

questions.texte: A student writes: 'If you will heat water to 100 degrees Celsius, it boils.' Is this sentence grammatically correct? Justify your answer by explaining the rule that was violated.
questions.reponse: No, the sentence is incorrect. The Zero Conditional is used for general truths and scientific facts. In Zero Conditional, we do not use 'will' in the 'if' clause. The correct sentence is: 'If you heat water to 100 degrees Celsius, it boils.'

intitule: Lesson 11 Assessment: Conditional Sentences - Application in Context

enonce.texte: Read the Cameroonian contexts below. Complete the conditional sentences using the correct structure (Zero, Type 1, or Type 2). Use the verbs in parentheses.
images: A busy market scene in a Cameroonian city like Douala or Yaoundé with sellers and buyers.
A drought-afflicted farmland in the North region of Cameroon with dry crops.

questions.texte: Use the cues to form logical conditional sentences.

sousQuestions.texte: If a seller (overcharge) you at the Mokolo market, you always (negotiate) the price.
If the government (invest) more in farming equipment, food production (increase) significantly. (The government currently has a limited budget.)
If you (not / study) for your English exam, you (not / get) a good grade. (The exam is next week.)
When a heavy storm (come), the drainage system in our neighborhood often (overflow).
If I (be) the Minister of Secondary Education, I (build) more equipped classrooms in rural areas. (I am not the Minister.)

questions.texte: Rewrite each sentence as a conditional sentence as indicated in brackets, keeping the same meaning.

sousQuestions.texte: Wash your hands regularly, or you will get sick. (Use: Type 1 conditional)
sousQuestions.reponse: If you don't wash your hands regularly, you will get sick.

sousQuestions.texte: I don't have a car, so I can't drive you to the airport. (Use: Type 2 conditional)
sousQuestions.reponse: If I had a car, I would drive you to the airport.

enonce.texte: Analyze the real-life problems described below. Use conditional sentences to formulate logical advice (Type 1) or to express imagined solutions (Type 2).
images: Teenagers standing outside a cybercafé in a Cameroonian town.
A sign showing 'HIV/AIDS Awareness Day' at a community health center.

questions.texte:
Situation A: 'My younger brother spends all his pocket money on video games and has nothing left for his school books.'

(a) Formulate a piece of realistic advice for your brother using a Type 1 conditional.
(b) Express an imagined, ideal solution for this problem using a Type 2 conditional.
questions.reponse:
(a) If you stop spending all your money on video games, you will have enough for your school books. OR If you buy fewer video games, you will save money for books.
(b) If my brother saved his money wisely, he would have no problem buying his books.

questions.texte:
Situation B: 'The rate of new HIV infections among young people in our community is still high despite prevention campaigns.'

(a) Propose a real solution using a Type 1 conditional based on voluntary testing.
(b) Hypothesize about a different outcome for the community using a Type 2 conditional, starting with 'If everyone...'
questions.reponse:
(a) If more young people go for voluntary testing, they will know their status and protect themselves and others.
(b) If everyone used protection and got tested regularly, the rate of new infections would drop dramatically.
